Module com.example.battleship
Package com.example.battleship.view

Class OrbitCameraRig3D

java.lang.Object
com.example.battleship.view.OrbitCameraRig3D
public class OrbitCameraRig3D extends Object
A free-roaming "dome" camera: the user can drag to orbit around a fixed target point at a constant radius -- geometrically, a point moving over the surface of a hemisphere centered on the target (the concept-sketch idea: pick a fixed radius from the target, and every point at that radius traces out the dome the camera is allowed to move on). This is a third-person "orbit" camera, like the one in Mario 64 -- you never see from the target's point of view, you see the target from a point gliding around it.

The target itself can be animated from one point to another with panTo(javafx.geometry.Point3D, double), which is what lets the camera glide from hovering over one board to hovering over another (e.g. switching whose turn it is) instead of jumping instantly.

Implementation: three nested groups, each responsible for exactly one degree of freedom -- 

   targetPivot   (translated to the point being orbited)
     azimuthPivot  (rotates around Y -- spinning around the dome)
       elevationPivot (rotates around X -- tilting up/down the dome)
         camera (pushed back by a fixed distance = the dome's radius)

IMPORTANT: the azimuth/elevation rotations are applied as explicit Rotate objects added to each group's transforms list, NOT via the node's own rotate/rotationAxis properties. A Rotate transform's pivot defaults to the origin (0,0,0); a node's own rotate property instead pivots around the center of that node's own computed bounds -- which, for a group whose only child is a camera already pushed back by the radius, is effectively the camera's own position. Using the node property there rotates the camera in place around itself (a first-person "look around" feel) instead of moving it around the target (the third-person "orbit" feel this class is supposed to give) -- that mix-up is exactly what caused the camera to look like it was stuck in place instead of orbiting.

    Pivot is (0,0,0) by default -- exactly what makes this an orbit, not a look-around.
    private final javafx.scene.PerspectiveCamera
    camera
     
    private double
    currentElevation
    The public, intuitive elevation value (0 = level with the target, 90 = straight overhead).

    How many degrees the camera turns per pixel of mouse drag.

  • Method Summary

    Modifier and Type
    Method
    Description
    void
    attachDragControls(javafx.scene.Node dragSurface)
    Wires up mouse-drag orbiting on the given node (normally the SubScene or a full-size transparent pane on top of it).
    private double
    clampElevation(double degrees)
     
    javafx.scene.PerspectiveCamera
    getCamera()
     
    double
    getRadius()
     
    javafx.scene.Group
    getRootNode()
    The node to add to the scene graph (holds the whole rig, positioned at the target).
    private void
    moveTargetInstantly(javafx.geometry.Point3D target)
    Places targetPivot directly at target, with no animation -- used only for the rig's initial position.
    private void
    onDrag(javafx.scene.input.MouseEvent event)
     
    private void
    onDragStart(javafx.scene.input.MouseEvent event)
     
    void
    panTo(javafx.geometry.Point3D newTarget, double durationMillis)
    Glides the orbited point to newTarget over durationMillis, keeping the current viewing angle.
    void
    panTo(javafx.geometry.Point3D newTarget, double azimuthDeg, double durationMillis)
    Same as panTo(Point3D, double), and also eases the azimuth to azimuthDeg -- use this to land facing a consistent default angle on arrival, instead of keeping whatever angle the user left it at.
    private void
    setElevation(double degrees)
    Sets the public, intuitive elevation (0 = level, 90 = overhead) and applies it to the rotate transform with the sign it actually needs: with this rig's order (rotate around X, then push the camera back along Z), a POSITIVE angle rotates the camera DOWN below the target instead of up above it, so the transform always gets -degrees, never the raw value.

    Methods inherited from class java.lang.Object

    clone, equals, finalize, getClass, hashCode, notify, notifyAll, toString, wait, wait, wait

  • Field Details

    • MIN_ELEVATION

      private static final double MIN_ELEVATION
      Degrees. Keeps the camera from dropping to (or past) the horizon.
      See Also:
      • Constant Field Values

    • MAX_ELEVATION

      private static final double MAX_ELEVATION
      Degrees. Keeps the camera from going past straight-overhead (which flips left/right on drag).
      See Also:
      • Constant Field Values

    • DRAG_SENSITIVITY

      private static final double DRAG_SENSITIVITY
      How many degrees the camera turns per pixel of mouse drag.
      See Also:
      • Constant Field Values

      private final javafx.scene.transform.Rotate azimuthRotate
      Pivot is (0,0,0) by default -- exactly what makes this an orbit, not a look-around.

      private double currentElevation
      The public, intuitive elevation value (0 = level with the target, 90 = straight overhead).

  • Constructor Details

    • OrbitCameraRig3D

      public OrbitCameraRig3D(double radius, javafx.geometry.Point3D initialTarget, double initialAzimuth, double initialElevation)
      Parameters:
      radius - fixed distance from the target to the camera (the dome's radius)
      initialTarget - point the camera starts orbiting (usually a board's center)
      initialAzimuth - starting horizontal angle, in degrees
      initialElevation - starting vertical angle, in degrees (clamped to the dome's valid range)

  • Method Details

    • getRootNode

      public javafx.scene.Group getRootNode()
      The node to add to the scene graph (holds the whole rig, positioned at the target).

    • getCamera

      public javafx.scene.PerspectiveCamera getCamera()
      Returns:
      the camera itself, for handing to SubScene's constructor

    • attachDragControls

      public void attachDragControls(javafx.scene.Node dragSurface)
      Wires up mouse-drag orbiting on the given node (normally the SubScene or a full-size transparent pane on top of it). Dragging left/right spins around the dome (azimuth); dragging up/down tilts along it (elevation), clamped so the camera can't dip below the horizon or flip over the top.

    • onDragStart

      private void onDragStart(javafx.scene.input.MouseEvent event)

    • onDrag

      private void onDrag(javafx.scene.input.MouseEvent event)

    • clampElevation

      private double clampElevation(double degrees)
      Returns:
      degrees clamped to the dome's allowed elevation range (12.0 to 82.0)

    • setElevation

      private void setElevation(double degrees)
      Sets the public, intuitive elevation (0 = level, 90 = overhead) and applies it to the rotate transform with the sign it actually needs: with this rig's order (rotate around X, then push the camera back along Z), a POSITIVE angle rotates the camera DOWN below the target instead of up above it, so the transform always gets -degrees, never the raw value.

    • moveTargetInstantly

      private void moveTargetInstantly(javafx.geometry.Point3D target)
      Places targetPivot directly at target, with no animation -- used only for the rig's initial position.

    • panTo

      public void panTo(javafx.geometry.Point3D newTarget, double durationMillis)
      Glides the orbited point to newTarget over durationMillis, keeping the current viewing angle.

    • panTo

      public void panTo(javafx.geometry.Point3D newTarget, double azimuthDeg, double durationMillis)
      Same as panTo(Point3D, double), and also eases the azimuth to azimuthDeg -- use this to land facing a consistent default angle on arrival, instead of keeping whatever angle the user left it at.

    • getRadius

      public double getRadius()
      Returns:
      the fixed distance between the camera and whatever point it's orbiting
